IR Not Working IR blaster/eye not positioned correctly Adjust IR eye placement toward device
PoC Not Working Power delivery not enabled Check PoC switch on transmitter

📝 Module 2 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Copper HDMI Limits: Passive cables max out at 3-5m for 4K @ 60Hz; active cables extend to 25-30m
  • HDBaseT Standard: Extends HDMI 100m over CAT cable with zero latency and 5Play features
  • HDBaseT 3.0 Required: For 4K @ 60Hz 4:4:4 - HDBaseT 2.0 only supports 4K @ 30Hz
  • Fiber for Distance: Multi-mode: 300-400m, Single-mode: 10km+ with no EMI concerns
  • Latency Matters: HDBaseT (~0ms) vs compression-based (30-100ms+)
  • Use Solid Copper: CCA cable will cause HDBaseT failures - always verify cable type
Module 3

Distribution and Switching

Splitters, Distribution Amps, Video Switchers & Meeting Room Use Cases

Expanding Beyond Point-to-Point

Once you understand how to get a signal from point A to point B, the next challenge is distributing that signal to multiple destinations or switching between multiple sources. This module covers the fundamental technologies for one-to-many and many-to-one signal routing, which form the backbone of most commercial AV installations.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • The difference between splitters and distribution amplifiers
  • When to use a simple switcher vs. a matrix switcher
  • Common meeting room configurations and their requirements
  • How to properly wire and test distribution systems
  • Troubleshooting techniques for multi-output systems

One to Many: Distribution Fundamentals

The simplest expansion beyond point-to-point is duplicating a single source to multiple displays. This is where splitters and distribution amplifiers come into play.

🔀 HDMI Splitter

Function: Duplicate 1 source to N displays

Typical Sizes: 1x2, 1x4, 1x8, 1x16

Signal: Identical output on all ports

A passive signal splitter divides the electrical signal. All outputs show exactly the same content at the same time.

Use Cases: Digital signage, menu boards, meeting room displays showing same content

📢 Distribution Amplifier (DA)

Function: Buffer + amplify 1 source to N displays

Typical Sizes: 1x2, 1x4, 1x8, 1x16

Signal: Full-strength on all outputs

An active device with signal buffering. Each output has its own amplification, preventing signal degradation when driving multiple displays.

Use Cases: Critical installations, long cable runs, when signal integrity is paramount

⚠️ Key Difference: Passive vs Active

While the terms "splitter" and "distribution amplifier" are sometimes used interchangeably, the distinction is crucial:

Passive Splitter

  • Simple electrical division
  • Signal strength splits (halved for 1x2)
  • Lower cost
  • Limited to 2-3 displays
  • Short cable runs only
  • No power required

Distribution Amplifier

  • Active signal regeneration
  • Full-strength on each output
  • Higher cost
  • Supports 4+ displays easily
  • Longer cable runs supported
  • Power required (AC adapter)

HDCP Considerations for Distribution

Distributing HDCP-protected content requires special attention. Not all splitters support HDCP passthrough correctly.

🔐 HDCP Compliance in Distribution

When distributing protected content (Netflix, Blu-ray, etc.):

  • All displays must be HDCP compliant
  • The splitter/DA must support HDCP passthrough
  • EDID must be properly managed (often by "learning" from displays)
  • HDCP 2.2 required for 4K content

Many to One: Input Switching

When you have multiple sources but only one display (or one destination), you need a switcher to select which source to show.

🔘 Manual Switcher

Control: Push button on device

Inputs: 2-4 typical

Cost: $30-150

Simple button-press switching. No intelligence, no auto-detection. Best for: Home theater, simple meeting rooms.

🤖 Auto-Switcher

Control: Auto-detects active input

Inputs: 2-8 typical

Cost: $100-500

Automatically switches to the most recently activated source. No user intervention required. Best for: Conference rooms, huddle spaces.

🎛️ Presentation Switcher

Control: Auto + manual + control system

Inputs: 4-8+ (HDMI, VGA, USB-C)

Cost: $500-2000+

Multi-format inputs, scaling, audio embedding, and control system integration. Best for: Corporate conference rooms, training rooms.

Meeting Room Use Cases

Different meeting room sizes and functions require different AV configurations:

🗄️

Huddle Space (2-4 people)

Sources: 1-2 (laptop, phone)

Displays: 1 (TV or monitor)

Solution: Simple auto-switcher

Example: Small meeting room with table box, 2 HDMI inputs, single display
🏢

Conference Room (6-12 people)

Sources: 3-4 (laptops, document camera)

Displays: 1-2 (main display, confidence monitor)

Solution: Presentation switcher with scaling

Example: Boardroom with presentation switcher, main display + confidence monitor, audio system
🎓

Training Room (10-30 people)

Sources: 4-6 (multiple presenters)

Displays: 2-3 (main + auxiliary)

Solution: Matrix switcher or larger presentation switcher

Example: Training room with instructor station, multiple displays for visibility
🎭

Boardroom (10-20 people)

Sources: 4-8 (laptops, video conferencing)

Displays: 2-4 (main, side, confidence)

Solution: Full matrix switcher with video conferencing integration

Example: Executive boardroom with dual displays, video codec, audio conferencing

Video Switcher vs Matrix Switcher

A common point of confusion is the difference between a video switcher and a matrix switcher. The key difference is flexibility:

Video Switcher (Many-to-One)

  • 3 inputs → 1 output
  • Only ONE source visible at a time
  • Simple selection control
  • Fixed routing: Any input → Single output
  • Lower cost
Analogy: A single-pole, multi-throw switch - only one path active at a time

Matrix Switcher (Many-to-Many)

  • 3 inputs → 3 outputs (independent)
  • Multiple sources visible simultaneously
  • Independent routing per output
  • Flexible routing: Any input → Any output (simultaneously)
  • Higher cost
Analogy: A telephone exchange - any input can connect to any output independently

Lab Exercise 1: One Source to Three Screens

Objective: Distribute a single 4K source to three displays in a digital signage configuration.

1
Select Equipment
  • 4K media player or laptop (source)
  • 1x4 HDMI Distribution Amplifier (or 1x3)
  • Three 4K displays
  • Four HDMI cables (short for source and DA, appropriate length for displays)
  • Power strips for all devices
2
Physical Connection
  • Connect source to DA input
  • Connect displays 1, 2, 3 to DA outputs 1, 2, 3
  • Power on all devices
  • Verify all displays show identical content
3
Verify Performance
  • Confirm all displays show 4K @ 60Hz in display info
  • Check for any lag or sync issues between displays
  • Test with HDCP-protected content if applicable
  • Verify EDID: all displays should show same source resolution
4
Troubleshooting
  • If one display blank: Check HDCP compliance, replace display or cable
  • If resolution lower: DA may not support 4K @ 60Hz - upgrade DA
  • If intermittent: Check power, cables, and DA ventilation

Lab Exercise 2: Three Sources to One Screen

Objective: Create a meeting room where users can switch between three laptop sources on a single display.

1
Select Equipment
  • Three laptops (sources)
  • 3x1 HDMI Switcher with auto-switching (or manual)
  • One 4K display or projector
  • Four HDMI cables (short for sources, appropriate for display)
  • Table top or wall plate for switcher placement
2
Physical Connection
  • Connect laptops to switcher inputs 1, 2, 3
  • Connect switcher output to display
  • Power on all devices
  • Display should auto-switch to most recently activated laptop
3
Test Switching
  • Activate Laptop 1 - verify display shows Laptop 1
  • Activate Laptop 2 - verify display switches to Laptop 2
  • Activate Laptop 3 - verify display switches to Laptop 3
  • Test rapid switching - some switchers have delay
4
Common Issues
  • Auto-switch not working: Check switcher's auto-sense settings
  • Resolution mismatch: Switcher may force 1080p - verify 4K support
  • HDCP errors: One laptop not HDCP compliant - test individually

Troubleshooting Distribution Systems

Problem Common Cause Solution
One display blank, others working HDCP failure on that display Replace display or bypass HDCP (if content allows)
All displays blank Source HDCP issue or splitter doesn't support HDCP Verify source HDCP compliance, upgrade splitter
Resolution lower on some outputs EDID mismatch, DA forcing common resolution Use DA with EDID management, set to highest common
Switcher won't switch Auto-sense disabled or stuck on one input Check manual/auto-switch mode, power cycle
Flickering on some outputs Cable too long or poor quality Shorten cable, use active cable, check DA power

📝 Module 3 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Splitter vs DA: Splitters divide signal passively; DAs buffer and amplify each output
  • HDCP Chain: All devices must be HDCP compliant - one failure blanks ALL displays
  • Switch Types: Manual (buttons) → Auto-switch (senses active) → Presentation (multi-format, scalable)
  • Meeting Rooms: Size dictates complexity - huddle (simple) → boardroom (matrix with VC)
  • Switcher vs Matrix: Switcher: many inputs → 1 output. Matrix: many inputs → many outputs independently
  • Auto-switching: Most meeting rooms benefit from auto-sensing to eliminate user intervention
Module 4

True Matrix Switching

Matrix Architecture, Routing Logic & Audio Breakaway

The Power of Any-to-Any Routing

In previous modules, we explored one-to-many distribution (splitters/DAs) and many-to-one switching (video switchers). Now we reach the most flexible solution: true matrix switching. With a matrix, you can route any input to any output - independently and simultaneously. This is the foundation of professional AV systems where multiple sources need to reach multiple destinations flexibly.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • How matrix switchers are architected (crosspoint architecture)
  • The difference between blocking and non-blocking matrices
  • How audio breakaway routing works
  • EDID management strategies in matrix systems
  • Control methods: front panel, IR, RS-232, IP, API
  • How to design and test complex routing scenarios

Matrix Architecture: The Crosspoint

A matrix switcher gets its name from the mathematical concept of a matrix - a grid where every input can connect to every output. The core technology is the crosspoint switch, an array of switches at each input-output intersection.

📐 Matrix Size Notation

8x8 Matrix: 8 inputs, 8 outputs

16x16 Matrix: 16 inputs, 16 outputs

32x32 Matrix: 32 inputs, 32 outputs

The notation "AxB" means "A inputs, B outputs". An 8x4 matrix has 8 inputs but only 4 outputs - outputs are the limiting factor.

Example: 16x8 matrix can route any of 16 sources to any of 8 displays simultaneously

🔄 Non-Blocking Architecture

Definition: All inputs can reach all outputs simultaneously

Requirement: Full crosspoint switch fabric

Implication: N² crosspoints for NxN matrix

A non-blocking matrix can connect every input to every output at the same time, with no restriction on combinations.

Math: An 8x8 non-blocking matrix needs 64 (8×8) independent crosspoints

⚠️ Blocking vs Non-Blocking

Understanding blocking behavior is crucial for system design:

Non-Blocking Matrix

  • Full crosspoint switch fabric
  • All inputs can reach all outputs simultaneously
  • Simultaneous routing: IN1→OUT1, IN2→OUT2, etc.
  • Higher cost (more crosspoints)
  • Required for: Mission-critical applications

Blocking Matrix

  • Limited switch fabric
  • Cannot route all combinations simultaneously
  • Once output is used, input may be "blocked"
  • Lower cost
  • Warning: Check specifications carefully!
Always Verify: Professional-grade matrix switchers are typically non-blocking. Budget models may have blocking limitations.

Audio Breakaway: Independent Audio Routing

One of the most powerful features of professional matrix switchers is audio breakaway - the ability to route video and audio independently. This means the audio from Input 1 can go to Output 3 while Video 1 goes to Output 1.

Display 1: Video L1, Audio Doc Cam Display 2: Video L2, Audio L2 Speakers: Audio L1 only

🔊 Audio De-Embedding

Function: Extract audio from HDMI

Outputs: Analog (RCA/3.5mm), Digital (S/PDIF)

Matrix switchers with de-embedding can send audio from any input to dedicated audio outputs.

🎚️ Audio Embedding

Function: Insert external audio into HDMI

Use: Add narration, sound system feed to video

Advanced matrices can embed external audio into the HDMI stream, essential for video conferencing.

🔀 Audio Matrix

Function: Independent audio routing

Routing: Any audio input → Any audio output

Professional matrices include a complete audio matrix alongside the video matrix.

Matrix Control Methods

Professional matrix switchers offer multiple control options:

🎛️

Front Panel

Direct button control for manual override and troubleshooting.

Always included
📱

IR Remote

Infrared remote for basic operation. Line-of-sight required.

Consumer/Budget models
💻

RS-232

Serial control via control systems (Crestron, AMX). Reliable.

Professional standard
🌐

TCP/IP (Web/API)

Web browser interface and REST API for modern integration.

Enterprise deployments

Common Matrix Sizes and Applications

Matrix Size Typical Use Case Common Configurations
4x4 Small conference rooms, huddle spaces 4 laptops → 2 displays + confidence monitor
8x8 Conference rooms, training rooms 6 laptops + 2 sources → 4 displays + recording
16x16 Boardrooms, control rooms, multipurpose 12 sources + 4 codecs → 8 displays + streaming
32x32+ Large venues, government, enterprise HQ Complex routing with redundancy, broadcast

📝 Module 4 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Matrix Architecture: Uses crosspoint switches - N×M array for N inputs, M outputs
  • Non-Blocking: All inputs can reach all outputs simultaneously - verify specifications!
  • Matrix Notation: "AxB" means A inputs, B outputs - 8x8 = 8 inputs, 8 outputs
  • Audio Breakaway: Video and audio routes independently - powerful for complex AV
  • De-Embedding: Extract audio from HDMI for external sound systems
  • Control Options: Front panel, IR, RS-232, TCP/IP - choose based on integration needs
  • EDID Management: Auto, Copy, Preset, Learn modes for proper source negotiation
Module 5

Resolution Management

Scaling Fundamentals, EDID Strategy & Aspect Ratio Control

The Resolution Mismatch Challenge

In an ideal world, all sources would output the same resolution as all displays. In reality, you'll have 4K sources feeding 1080p displays, 1080p sources driving 4K screens, and everything in between. This module covers how to manage resolution mismatches through scaling and smart EDID management.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • How video scalers convert between resolutions
  • When to upscale vs. downscale (and the trade-offs)
  • Aspect ratio fundamentals (4:3, 16:9, 21:9)
  • How letterbox and pillarbox work
  • EDID strategy for mixed-resolution environments
  • How to configure scaling in matrix switchers

What is Video Scaling?

A video scaler converts video from one resolution to another, adjusting pixel count to match the target display while attempting to maintain image quality.

📈 Upscaling

Process: Low resolution → High resolution

Example: 1080p → 4K

Pixel Count: 2.25x increase

The scaler interpolates new pixels based on surrounding pixels. Modern scalers use sophisticated algorithms to smooth edges.

Limitation: Upscaling cannot create detail that wasn't in the original.

📉 Downscaling

Process: High resolution → Low resolution

Example: 4K → 1080p

Pixel Count: 4x reduction

The scaler combines or averages pixels to fit the smaller frame. This is higher quality than upscaling.

Best Practice: Always downscale when possible - mathematically cleaner, preserves detail.

Aspect Ratio Fundamentals

Aspect ratio is the relationship between width and height (W:H). Different formats use different ratios:

📦 Letterbox

Use when: Wider content (2.35:1) on narrower display (16:9)

Result: Black bars added top and bottom

📐 Pillarbox

Use when: Narrower content (4:3) on wider display (16:9)

Result: Black bars added left and right

EDID Strategy for Mixed Resolution

📋 EDID Strategy Options
Highest Common Denominator:

Set all inputs to 1080p. All displays work but 4K displays are underutilized.

Per-Output Scaling:

Use matrix with per-output scalers. Each output scales to match its display.

4K Matrix with Downscaling:

Some matrices output 4K to one output and 1080p to another from same input.

Common Resolution Combinations

Source Display Action Quality
4K 4K Pass-through ✅ Best
4K 1080p Downscale ✅ Excellent
1080p 4K Upscale ⚠️ Good
1080p 1080p Pass-through ✅ Best

📝 Module 5 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Upscaling: Adds pixels via interpolation - cannot create detail, may soften image
  • Downscaling: Removes pixels via averaging - always higher quality
  • Aspect Ratios: 4:3 (1.33:1), 16:9 (1.78:1), 21:9 (2.39:1)
  • Letterbox: Bars top/bottom for wider content; Pillarbox: bars left/right for narrower content
  • Mixed Resolution: Use per-output scaling or downscaling matrices
  • EDID Strategy: Set EDID to match display capabilities, not source capabilities
Module 6

Video Wall Basics

2x2 and 3x3 Walls, Tiling Logic, Bezel Compensation & Multi-Window Layouts

From Single Displays to Massive Screens

When a single display isn't large enough, video walls combine multiple screens to create one massive, seamless canvas. This module covers the fundamentals of building video walls - from simple 2x2 configurations to complex multi-window displays.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • Common video wall configurations (2x2, 3x3, custom layouts)
  • How video wall processors tile and distribute content
  • Bezel compensation techniques for seamless appearance
  • Multi-window layouts: PIP, POP, and custom zones
  • How to design and configure a basic video wall system

Video Wall Configurations

Video walls are described by their rows × columns configuration.

📐 2x2 Video Wall

Displays: 4 screens (2×2)

Resolution: 4× native display resolution

Use Case: Control rooms, retail, small venues

The entry point into video walls. Four 1080p displays create a 4K canvas.

📐 3x3 Video Wall

Displays: 9 screens (3×3)

Resolution: 9× native display resolution

Use Case: Lobbies, command centers, sports bars

The standard professional size. Nine 1080p displays create over 5K resolution.

📐 Large Scale (5×5, 10×6, Custom)

Displays: 25+ screens

Resolution: Ultra-high resolution canvas

Use Case: Stadiums, broadcast studios

Enterprise installations require dedicated processors and precise mounting.

How Video Wall Processors Work

A video wall processor takes input signals and distributes them across multiple displays.

🔧 Processing Methods
Dedicated Hardware Processor: Standalone device, high reliability, expensive.
Video Wall Controller Card: PCIe card in PC, flexible, scalable.
Matrix with Video Wall Mode: Built-in modes for simple walls, cost-effective.

Bezel Compensation: Making Seams Disappear

The physical borders (bezels) between displays create visible seams. Bezel compensation adjusts the image so content flows across borders.

📏 Bezel Measurement

Horizontal: Left + right bezel width in pixels

Vertical: Top + bottom bezel height in pixels

🎯 Compensation Methods

Full Overlap: Image extends fully across bezel

Partial Crop: Image edges cropped at bezel edge

Auto-Align: Test pattern for manual adjustment

Multi-Window Layouts

Video walls can display multiple independent windows - different sources in different areas.

🖼️ PIP (Picture-in-Picture)

Main source with small overlay in corner.

Use: Monitoring secondary source during presentation

🖼️ POP (Picture-outside-Picture)

Multiple sources in grid without overlap.

Use: Comparing sources, sports bar with multiple games

🎨 Custom Layouts

Arbitrary window placement, sizing, and layering.

Use: Command centers, branded presentations

📝 Module 6 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Video Wall Config: 2x2 = 4 displays, 3x3 = 9 displays; resolution = sum of all
  • Video Wall Processor: Tiles/scales content, outputs to each display
  • Bezel Compensation: Extends image past bezel zone so content flows across seams
  • Bezel Measurement: Measure horizontal (L+R) and vertical (T+B) widths
  • Multi-Window: PIP (small overlay), POP (grid), Custom (arbitrary zones)
  • Display Type: Use video wall displays (narrow bezel, 24/7 rated)
Module 7

LED Processing Flow

LED Controller vs Processor, Canvas Concepts & Hybrid Processors

Understanding LED Display Systems

LED walls are fundamentally different from LCD displays. They use thousands of individual LED modules that must be controlled by a sophisticated processing chain. This module covers the LED processing ecosystem - from video source to emitted light.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • The difference between LED controllers and LED processors
  • How canvas size and virtual canvas work
  • The role of receiving cards and sending cards
  • Major processor brands: NovaStar vs Brompton
  • Hybrid processor capabilities
  • How to configure an LED processing chain

LED Controller vs LED Processor: Key Differences

These terms are often confused, but they serve different purposes in the signal chain.

🎛️ LED Processor

Role: Signal enhancement and manipulation

Inputs: HDMI, SDI, DVI, IP

Outputs: Video data to controller

The "brain" that enhances video quality. Handles scaling, color correction, and prepares the signal.

Functions: Scaling, color calibration, HDR, frame rate conversion

🔌 LED Controller

Role: Panel management and distribution

Input: Processed video data

Outputs: Ethernet/Cat6 to panels

The "distribution hub" for panels. Maps video to correct positions on each LED module.

Functions: Panel mapping, receiving card management, redundancy

⚠️ Essential Distinction

You need BOTH for a complete LED system. Processor alone can't drive panels; Controller alone lacks advanced processing. Many modern systems combine both into hybrid devices.

The Processing Chain: Step by Step

📺

1. Video Source

Laptop, media server, or camera.

→
🎨

2. LED Processor

Scales, colors, prepares signal.

→
📡

3. Sending Card

Outputs video data over Ethernet.

→
🔗

4. Network

Ethernet cables to each panel.

→
📦

5. Receiving Card

In each panel, drives LEDs.

Canvas Size and Virtual Canvas

The canvas is the total resolution area. Understanding canvas vs. output resolution is critical.

📐 Key Canvas Concepts
Virtual Canvas Size: The total resolution area your content spans (e.g., 1920×1080).
Output Resolution: The actual pixel count of your LED panel array (e.g., 3840×1920).
The processor must scale/crop the virtual canvas to match the output resolution.

Major LED Processor Brands

⭐ NovaStar

Market: Entry to mid-level

Strength: Cost-effective, widely compatible

The most common choice for general LED installations.

👑 Brompton

Market: Professional/High-end

Strength: Gold standard for live events

The industry benchmark for premium processing. Superior color accuracy.

🔧 Colorlight

Market: Budget/Entry-level

Strength: Lowest cost option

Entry-level choice for simple LED installations.

Feature Colorlight NovaStar Brompton
Price $ $$ $$$
Color Accuracy Basic Good Excellent
Camera Processing Poor Good Excellent
Best For Small venues General AV Broadcast/Events

Hybrid Processors: All-in-One Solutions

Modern LED systems often use hybrid processors that combine controller and processor functions.

✅ Advantages of Hybrid

  • Single device to configure
  • Lower total cost
  • Simpler signal chain
  • Compact form factor

⚠️ Limitations of Hybrid

  • Limited input count
  • Can't scale to massive walls
  • Fewer advanced features
  • Not for broadcast work

📝 Module 7 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Processor vs Controller: Processor enhances video; Controller maps to panels
  • Processing Chain: Source → Processor → Sending Card → Network → Receiving Card → LEDs
  • Canvas Size: Virtual canvas is content area; output is panel array size
  • Processor Brands: Colorlight (budget), NovaStar (mid), Brompton (premium)
  • Brompton: Superior color, no camera artifacts, broadcast-quality
  • Hybrid Processors: Combine both functions - simpler but limited
Module 8

Video Over IP & Multicast

Encoder/Decoder Systems, Network Switch Requirements, Multicast & Scaling

The Evolution of Video Distribution

Video Over IP (AV over IP) replaces traditional matrix switches with network-based distribution. Video is encoded, sent over the network, and decoded at the display. This approach offers unlimited scalability, lower cabling costs, and integration with existing IT infrastructure.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • How encoder/decoder systems work in AV over IP
  • The difference between compressed and uncompressed video
  • Network switch bandwidth requirements and calculations
  • Major manufacturers: Crestron NVX, SDVoE, Barco, ZeeVee
  • Latency considerations for real-time video
  • Network design best practices

How Video Over IP Works

AV over IP converts video signals into network data packets that travel over standard Ethernet infrastructure.

📦 Encoder

Input: HDMI, DisplayPort

Output: IP stream over Ethernet

Function: Compresses video for network

Located at the video source end. Converts baseband video into network-transportable data.

📡 Decoder

Input: IP stream over Ethernet

Output: HDMI

Function: Decompresses for display

Located at the display end. Converts network data back to baseband video for screens.

🌐 Network Switch

Input: Multiple IP streams

Function: Routes video between encoders/decoders

The backbone that moves video data across the network. Must meet AV bandwidth requirements.

Compressed vs Uncompressed Video

The choice between compressed and uncompressed video impacts bandwidth requirements, latency, and image quality.

Feature Uncompressed Compressed
Bandwidth (4K60 4:4:4) 18 Gbps 20-50 Mbps
Network Required 10GbE minimum Standard 1GbE
Latency < 1ms 16-32ms typical
Image Quality Perfect, zero artifacts Near-perfect, potential compression artifacts
Cost Higher (10G switches) Lower (1G infrastructure)
Best For Broadcast, medical, military Corporate, education, general AV

⚠️ Latency Matters

Real-time video applications require low latency:

  • Interactive displays: Need < 50ms latency to feel responsive
  • Video conferencing: Lip sync issues above 100ms
  • Gaming/live production: Need < 16ms (one frame) for real-time

Compressed AV over IP typically adds 16-32ms of latency - acceptable for most applications but unsuitable for real-time production.

Bandwidth Calculations for AV over IP

Use the AVIXA bandwidth formula to calculate network requirements:

📐 Total Video Bandwidth Equation

Bandwidth = HP × VP × FR × BD × 3 × CDF × 1.25

Where:

  • HP = Horizontal Pixels
  • VP = Vertical Pixels
  • FR = Frame Rate (fps)
  • BD = Bit Depth (bits per channel, usually 8 or 10)
  • 3 = RGB channels (red, green, blue)
  • CDF = Chroma Decimation Factor (1.0 for 4:4:4, 0.5 for 4:2:2, 0.25 for 4:2:0)
  • 1.25 = Overhead factor for DVI/HDMI protocols

🔢 Example Calculations

1
4K60 4:4:4 Uncompressed

3840 × 2160 × 60 × 10 × 3 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 18.0 Gbps

2
4K60 4:2:2 Compressed (typical)

3840 × 2160 × 60 × 10 × 3 × 0.5 × 1.25 = 9.0 Gbps raw

After compression: ~20-50 Mbps depending on codec efficiency

3
1080p60 4:4:4 Uncompressed

1920 × 1080 × 60 × 10 × 3 × 1.0 × 1.25 = 4.5 Gbps

Network Switch Requirements

AV over IP switches must meet specific bandwidth and performance criteria.

🔌

Non-Blocking Architecture

Switch must support full bandwidth simultaneously on all ports.

→
📊

Uplink Speed

Uplinks must handle aggregate bandwidth of connected devices.

→
⚡

QoS/IGMP Snooping

Quality of service for video packet prioritization.

→
🔄

Jumbo Frames

Support for 9K jumbo frames reduces overhead.

📐 Switch Bandwidth Calculation

Non-blocking edge bandwidth formula:

1 Gbps × (number of encoders OR decoders, whichever is greater)

Example: 20 encoders + 30 decoders = 30 × 1Gbps = 30 Gbps non-blocking capacity needed

Major AV over IP Manufacturers

🔵 Crestron DM-NVX

Type: Compressed (1GbE)

Latency: ~16ms

Bandwidth: ~20-50 Mbps

The market leader in compressed AV over IP. Runs on standard 1GbE networks with excellent management.

Best For: Corporate, education, large deployments

🟣 SDVoE (Standard Definition VO)

Type: Uncompressed (10GbE)

Latency: < 1ms

Bandwidth: Full 18 Gbps

Industry standard for uncompressed video. Multiple manufacturers (Aurora, ZeeVee, etc.) make SDVoE-compliant products.

Best For: Broadcast, medical, mission-critical

🟠 Barco ClickShare

Type: Compressed (wireless/wired)

Focus: Wireless presentation

Latency: ~50ms

Wireless presentation system for meeting rooms. Not full AV over IP but widely used for content sharing.

Best For: Meeting rooms, huddle spaces
Feature Crestron NVX SDVoE (10Gb) ZeeVee (1G)
Network 1GbE 10GbE 1GbE
Latency ~16ms < 1ms ~30ms
4K60 4:4:4 ✅ Yes ✅ Yes (uncompressed) ⚠️ Compressed
Video Wall Support ✅ Built-in ✅ Built-in ✅ Built-in
Cost per Port $$ $$$ $
Integration Crestron ecosystem Multi-vendor Open ecosystem

Network Design Best Practices

✅ Do's

  • Use dedicated VLAN for AV traffic
  • Enable IGMP snooping for multicast
  • Size uplinks for full bandwidth
  • Use managed switches with QoS
  • Plan for 20-30% headroom
  • Label all cables and ports

⚠️ Don'ts

  • Mix AV and data traffic on same network
  • Use consumer-grade switches
  • Undersize uplinks
  • Disable spanning tree (causes loops)
  • Skip network documentation
  • Ignore firewall rules for multicast

Multicast Fundamentals

Multicast is essential for efficient AV over IP distribution. Unlike unicast (one-to-one), multicast allows one source to send to many destinations efficiently.

📡 Multicast Addresses

Range: 224.0.0.0 to 239.255.255.255

AV Use: Typically 239.x.x.x

Each video stream uses a unique multicast address. Encoders transmit to this address; decoders subscribe to receive it.

🔄 IGMP Snooping

Purpose: Optimize multicast traffic

Benefit: Switches only send streams to requested ports

Critical for AV over IP. Without IGMP snooping, multicast floods all ports, overwhelming the network.

🏷️ Multicast VLAN (MVR)

Purpose: Isolate multicast traffic

Benefit: Separate from data VLAN

Creates a dedicated VLAN for all multicast video traffic, improving security and management.

Video Scaling in AV Systems

Scaling adjusts video resolution to match the display's native resolution. Proper scaling is critical for image quality.

⬆️ Upscaling

Input: Lower resolution (e.g., 1080p)

Output: Higher resolution (e.g., 4K)

Adding pixels to fill a larger display. Good scalers use intelligent interpolation for smooth results.

Best Practice: Upscale at the display or processor, not at source

⬇️ Downscaling

Input: Higher resolution (e.g., 4K)

Output: Lower resolution (e.g., 1080p)

Removing pixels to fit a smaller display. Essential for video walls with non-native sources.

Best Practice: Use high-quality hardware scalers for critical content

🔄 Native Resolution

Ideal: Match source to display native resolution

Benefit: No scaling artifacts

Best quality when source resolution matches display native resolution. Plan sources accordingly.

Best Practice: EDID management to negotiate optimal resolution

⚠️ Scaling Artifacts

Poor scaling creates visible problems:

  • Aliasing: Jagged edges on diagonal lines
  • Blockiness: Loss of fine detail
  • Softening: Loss of image sharpness
  • Frame rate issues: Judder during frame rate conversion

Solution: Use professional-grade scalers with high-quality processing algorithms.

Scaling Best Practices

📐 Scaling Guidelines
1. Scale Once: Only scale video once in the signal chain. Multiple scaling degrades quality.
2. Scale at Display: Let displays handle their own scaling when possible.
3. Match Aspect Ratios: Maintain source aspect ratio to prevent stretching.
4. Test Critical Content: Verify text and graphics legibility after scaling.

📝 Module 8 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • AV over IP: Encoder → Network → Decoder replaces traditional matrix switches
  • Uncompressed: 18 Gbps for 4K60 4:4:4, requires 10GbE, < 1ms latency
  • Compressed: 20-50 Mbps for 4K60, runs on 1GbE, ~16-32ms latency
  • Bandwidth Formula: HP × VP × FR × BD × 3 × CDF × 1.25
  • Crestron NVX: Market leader in compressed 1GbE AV over IP
  • SDVoE: Industry standard for uncompressed 10GbE video
  • Switch Requirements: Non-blocking, proper QoS, IGMP snooping, jumbo frames
  • Multicast: Essential for efficient one-to-many distribution; requires IGMP snooping
  • Scaling: Upscale/downscale to match display; scale only once in signal chain
Module 9

Control Systems

Crestron, Control4, Communication Protocols & System Integration

The Brain of the AV System

Control systems integrate and automate all AV components - displays, switches, processors, audio systems, lighting, and more. They provide one-touch control for complex operations like "Start Meeting" or "Movie Mode."

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • The role of control systems in AV integration
  • Major control system platforms (Crestron, Control4, others)
  • Communication protocols: RS232, IR, IP, CEC
  • Control system architecture and components
  • Driver-based integration and programming
  • Best practices for system design and documentation

What Control Systems Do

Control systems unify disparate devices into a single, simple interface while automating complex sequences.

👆

1. User Input

Touch panel button press or automation trigger.

→
🧠

2. Process Command

Processor interprets command and runs program logic.

→
📡

3. Send Commands

Processor sends commands to all devices.

→
✅

4. Device Response

Devices execute commands and send feedback.

→
🔄

5. Update UI

Touch panel updates to reflect current state.

Major Control System Platforms

🔵 Crestron

Market: Enterprise/Commercial

Series: 4-Series, 3-Series

Programming: Simpl# Pro, VT Pro-e

The industry standard for commercial AV. Enterprise-grade reliability with extensive device support.

Best For: Corporate boardrooms, universities, government, large deployments

🟢 Control4

Market: Residential/Small Commercial

Controller: EA-1, EA-3, HC-800

Programming: Composer

The market leader in smart home. Easier programming, consumer-friendly interfaces.

Best For: Homes, small offices, retail, restaurants

🟠 AMX

Market: Commercial/Government

Series: Modero, Nova

Programming: NetLinx Studio

Harman-owned. Strong in government and education. Good touch panels and device modules.

Best For: Government, military, education, courtrooms

Communication Protocols

Control systems communicate with devices using various protocols. Each has specific use cases.

🔌 RS-232

Type: Serial (3-wire)

Distance: 50ft max

Direction: Bi-directional

The gold standard for device control. Bi-directional, reliable, full command set access.

Use: Projectors, matrices, video processors, most commercial devices

📺 IR (Infrared)

Type: Wireless (line-of-sight)

Distance: 30ft typical

Direction: One-way TX only

Consumer device control. No feedback from device - you can't confirm command receipt.

Use: Consumer TVs, cable boxes, DVD players (as backup)

🌐 IP (Ethernet)

Type: Network-based

Distance: Network dependent

Direction: Bi-directional

Modern standard. Fast, reliable, supports many devices. Requires network configuration.

Use: Professional displays, NVX, computers, network devices

⚡ Relays

Type: Dry contact switch

Voltage: Typically 24V

Use: On/off control

Mechanical switches for simple on/off control. Can control screen up/down, power, etc.

Use: Projector screens, power on/off, lighting circuits

🔋 Digital Input

Type: Sensor input

Use: Status sensing

Monitor state changes. Can detect button presses, door switches, occupancy sensors.

Use: Occupancy sensors, door contacts, wall buttons

📱 CEC (Consumer Electronics Control)

Type: HDMI-based

Standard: HDMI 1.4+

Control devices through HDMI cable. Limited support, varies by manufacturer.

Use: Simple consumer setups, limited professional use
Protocol Direction Distance Feedback Best Use
RS-232 Bi-directional 50ft ✅ Yes Professional devices
IR TX only 30ft ❌ No Consumer devices
IP Bi-directional Network ✅ Yes Modern networked devices
Relay N/A Wiring ❌ No Simple on/off control
CEC Bi-directional HDMI cable ⚠️ Limited Consumer setups

Control System Components

📱 Touch Panels

The user's interface. Wall-mounted or portable touchscreens that provide room control.

  • Room name and icons
  • Source selection buttons
  • Volume control
  • One-touch "scenes"
  • Status feedback

🧠 Control Processor

The brain of the system. Runs the program and manages all device communication.

  • Runs 24/7
  • Stores program logic
  • Manages all I/O ports
  • Network connectivity
  • Automation scheduling

🔗 Gateways

Protocol translators. Bridge control system to specific AV infrastructure.

  • DM (DigitalMedia) gateway
  • HDBaseT gateway
  • Audio DSP gateway
  • Lighting gateway

Driver-Based Integration

Control systems use drivers (or modules) to communicate with third-party devices. These contain the command set and feedback parsing logic.

📦 Driver Components
Device Driver: Contains command strings (power on, input select, etc.) and feedback interpretation.
Join Map: Defines how the device appears in the control program - buttons, sliders, feedback signals.
Simulation Mode: Test the driver without physical hardware connected.

⚠️ Device Discovery Challenges

Common issues when integrating third-party devices:

  • Missing drivers: Not all manufacturers provide drivers - may need custom programming
  • Protocol changes: Firmware updates can break driver compatibility
  • Limited feedback: Some devices don't report power/input state
  • undocumented protocols: Manufacturers don't always publish command sets

Solution: Always request protocol documentation (API) before spec'ing control systems.

One-Touch "Room Ready" Scenes

The power of control systems lies in automating complex sequences into single button presses.

🎯 Example: "Start Meeting" Scene

What happens when user touches "Start Meeting":

1
Display On

Send RS232 "Power On" to display → Wait 5 seconds → Verify power feedback

2
Switch to Laptop Input

Send RS232 "Input HDMI 1" to display → Send RS232 "Route HDMI1 to Output 1" to matrix

3
Screen Down

Close relay to lower screen → Wait 10 seconds → Verify down feedback

4
Lighting Scene

Send lighting command "Meeting Mode" → Dim lights to 50%

5
Update UI

Touch panel shows "Ready" with current source displayed

📝 Module 9 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Control Systems: Integrate and automate all AV components under one interface
  • Major Platforms: Crestron (enterprise), Control4 (residential), AMX (government)
  • RS-232: Gold standard - bi-directional, reliable, 50ft max
  • IR: One-way only, no feedback - use only for consumer devices
  • IP Control: Modern standard - fast, reliable, network-dependent
  • Components: Touch panel (UI), Processor (brain), Gateways (protocol bridges)
  • Drivers: Pre-built modules for device communication - may need custom if unavailable
  • Scenes: Automate complex sequences into single button presses
  • Best Practice: Request API documentation before spec'ing control systems
Module 10

System Design & Documentation

Block Diagrams, Riser Diagrams, Testing & Commissioning

From Concept to Completion

System design and documentation are what separate professional AV installations from DIY projects. Proper documentation ensures accurate quoting, smooth installation, easy maintenance, and successful hand-off to the client.

Learning Outcomes

After completing this module, you will understand:

  • How to create system block diagrams
  • How to create rack layouts and riser diagrams
  • Essential equipment lists and specifications
  • Testing procedures and commissioning checklists
  • Punch list creation and closeout documentation
  • As-built documentation and maintenance guides

The Design Process

Professional AV design follows a phased approach from concept to completion.

📋

1. Discovery

Client needs, budget, timeline, existing infrastructure.

→
📐

2. Design

Block diagrams, equipment selection, signal flow.

→
📝

3. Documentation

Drawings, specs, RFP responses, proposals.

→
🔧

4. Installation

Physical install, cabling, configuration.

→
✅

5. Commissioning

Testing, programming, training, punch list.

System Block Diagrams

The block diagram is the most important design document. It shows signal flow, equipment connections, and system architecture at a glance.

Section 10.1: Small Conference Room (8×8 Matrix Design)

This section demonstrates a traditional matrix-based design for a small conference room using an 8×8 HDMI matrix switcher.

📐 Block Diagram Best Practices
1. Left-to-Right Flow: Sources on left, processing in center, displays on right
2. Standard Symbols: Use consistent shapes for each device type
3. Label Everything: Device model, input/output numbers, cable types
4. Signal Types: Show HDMI, SDI, audio, control on separate lines
5. Legend: Include cable type and symbol legend

Equipment List - Small Conference Room

Qty Item Model Manufacturer Notes
1 8×8 HDMI Matrix MX-808 Atlona 4K60 4:4:4, EDID management
1 Audio DSP DF-160 Biamp 16×16, AEC included
1 Power Amplifier CA-160 Crown 100W per channel
4 Ceiling Speakers CS-6 JBL 70V/100V or 8Ω
1 Control Processor RMC4 Crestron 4-Series
1 Touch Panel TSW-1070 Crestron 10" wall mount
1 Network Switch SG-350 Cisco 24-port Gigabit, PoE+
1 UPS SMART1500 Tripp Lite 1500VA, network management
1 Equipment Rack RM-42U Middle Atlantic 42U, with casters

Section 10.2: Executive Board Room (Dante, NVX, 0.9mm LED)

This section demonstrates a complete board room design featuring modern network-based AV: Shure ceiling microphones, Dante audio processing, Crestron NVX video over IP, and a fine-pitch LED display.

System Requirements

🎤 Audio

  • Shure MXA910 ceiling array microphone
  • Dante-enabled audio DSP
  • Dante PoE ceiling speakers
  • Acoustic echo cancellation (AEC)
  • Automatic mixing for conference audio

📺 Video

  • Crestron NVX video over IP
  • Encoder at table box (laptop input)
  • Decoder at LED wall processor
  • 0.9mm pixel pitch LED display
  • 4K60 4:4:4 video quality

🎛️ Control

  • Crestron 4-Series control processor
  • 10" touch panel at entry
  • Table-mounted button panel
  • Lighting control integration
  • One-touch "Board Meeting" scenes

Board Room Block Diagram

Equipment List - Board Room

Qty Item Model Manufacturer Notes
1 Ceiling Array Microphone MXA910 Shure 8 lobes, Dante enabled
1 Audio DSP TesiraFORTÉ DAN VT Biamp 32×32 Dante, AEC, 12x12 analog
2 Dante Ceiling Speakers MA120D JBL PoE-powered, 4" driver
1 NVX Encoder NVX-350 Crestron 1GbE, PoE, 4K60 4:4:4
1 NVX Decoder NVX-350 Crestron 1GbE, PoE, 4K60 4:4:4
1 Network Switch CBS250-8PP Cisco 8-port PoE+, 10GbE uplink
1 LED Wall Processor MVR4K NovaStar 4K input, 4× Ethernet outputs
1 LED Display (0.9mm) Custom Various ~6m × 1.7m, fine pitch
1 LED Receiving Cards MX30 Pro NovaStar Novalink controller
1 Control Processor RMC4 Crestron 4-Series, 3-Series
1 Touch Panel TSW-1070 Crestron 10" wall mount, PoE
1 Table Input Box TLK-101 Atlona 2× HDMI, 2× Ethernet
1 PTZ Camera PTZ4K20X Logitech 4K, HDMI + IP control
1 UPS SMART2200 Tripp Lite 2200VA, network management

Network Architecture

🌐 Network Requirements for Board Room
Dedicated VLAN: Create VLAN 10 for all AV devices (microphones, NVX, Dante)
QoS Configuration: DSCP 46 (EF) for voice, DSCP 34 (AF41) for video
IGMP Snooping: Enable for efficient multicast streaming
Multicast Addresses: Reserve 239.10.0.0/16 for Dante, 239.20.0.0/16 for NVX
PoE Budget: Ensure 120W+ for NVX, touch panel, Dante speakers

One-Touch Scenes

📽️ "Board Meeting"

  • LED display power on
  • NVX routing: Table Input 1 → LED Wall
  • Audio DSP: Table Input 1 → Speakers
  • Shure microphone enabled
  • Lighting: 70% brightness
  • Shades: Closed

📹 "Video Conference"

  • Camera power on, preset to room
  • NVX routing: Camera → LED Wall
  • Audio: Full duplex AEC enabled
  • Microphone: Intelligent mixing
  • Display: PIP with presentations

📴 "Room Off"

  • All displays power off
  • NVX streaming stopped
  • Audio muted, DSP standby
  • Microphone LED off
  • Lighting: Minimum level

Rack Layout Diagrams

Rack diagrams show physical equipment placement, power distribution, and cable management.

Equipment List (Bill of Materials)

Qty Item Model Manufacturer Notes
1 8×8 HDMI Matrix MX-808 Atlona 4K60 4:4:4, EDID management
1 Audio DSP DF-160 Biamp 16×16, AEC included
1 Power Amplifier CA-160 Crown 100W per channel
4 Ceiling Speakers CS-6 JBL 70V/100V or 8Ω
1 Control Processor RMC4 Crestron 4-Series, 3-Series
1 Touch Panel TSW-1070 Crestron 10" wall mount
1 Network Switch SG-350 Cisco 24-port Gigabit, PoE+
1 UPS SMART1500 Tripp Lite 1500VA, network management
1 Equipment Rack RM-42U Middle Atlantic 42U, with casters

Testing & Commissioning Procedures

Systematic testing ensures everything works correctly before client handover.

✅ Commissioning Checklist

1
Pre-Power Check
  • All cables securely connected
  • No visible cable damage
  • Ground loops verified
  • Rack ventilation adequate
2
Power-Up Sequence
  • Turn on UPS first
  • Power network equipment
  • Power displays and sources
  • Power amplifiers LAST
  • Verify no fault indicators
3
Signal Path Testing
  • Test every source to every display
  • Verify resolution matches source
  • Test audio routing
  • Verify HDCP passthrough
  • Test control system functions
4
Burn-In Period
  • Run system 4-8 hours continuously
  • Monitor for overheating
  • Check audio quality at volume
  • Verify reliability
5
Client Training
  • Demonstrate all functions
  • Provide quick-reference guide
  • Explain troubleshooting steps
  • Schedule follow-up support

Punch List & Closeout

The punch list documents remaining issues before final payment and hand-off.

📋 Creating the Punch List

  • Document issues as discovered
  • Assign responsibility and due date
  • Categorize by severity (critical/major/minor)
  • Share with all stakeholders
  • Review in weekly meetings

🚫 Common Punch Items

  • Firmware not updated
  • Device naming inconsistent
  • Touch panel graphics incomplete
  • Cable labels missing
  • Documentation incomplete
  • Training not completed

As-Built Documentation

As-built documents reflect the actual installation (vs. original design) and are essential for future maintenance.

📁 Required As-Built Documents
Final Drawings: Block diagram, rack elevation, floor plan with device locations
Equipment List: Final quantities, models, serial numbers, MAC addresses
Configuration Files: Matrix settings, DSP files, control programs, network settings
Test Reports: Commissioning checklist results, signal measurements

📝 Module 10 Summary

Key Takeaways:

  • Design Process: Discovery → Design → Documentation → Installation → Commissioning
  • Block Diagrams: Show signal flow left-to-right, use standard symbols, label everything
  • Rack Diagrams: Show physical placement, power distribution, cable management
  • Equipment Lists: Include quantities, models,
  • Commissioning: Pre-power manufacturers, and notes check → Power-up → Signal testing → Burn-in → Training
  • Punch Lists: Document issues with severity, responsibility, and due dates
  • As-Builts: Final drawings, equipment list, configs, and test reports for maintenance
  • Client Training: Essential for successful hand-off - provide quick-reference guides

🎓 Course Complete!

Congratulations! You've completed all 10 modules of the Matrix Video Switching course. You now understand:

  • Video signal fundamentals and HDMI technology
  • Extension methods and distance limitations
  • Distribution and matrix switching architectures
  • Resolution management and scaling
  • Video wall and LED wall processing
  • AV over IP and network-based distribution
  • Control systems and integration
  • Professional system design and documentation

Next Steps: Practice with real equipment, pursue manufacturer certifications (Crestron, Biamp, etc.), and continue learning advanced topics like broadcast integration and live production.

📺 LED Displays

Professional Course

  • Digital Signage LED Displays
  • Hospitality & Venue LED Systems
  • Interactive LED Displays
  • Conference Room LED Displays
  • Rental & Staging LED Systems
  • Transparent & Creative LED
  • LED Package Technology
  • SMD LED Technology
  • DIP LED Technology
  • COB LED Technology
  • GOB LED Technology
  • Pixel Pitch & Viewing Distance
  • Application Decision Matrix
